Annual Exam - 2020

Code: 65/3/2 (set-2)

  1. Please check that this question paper contains 15 printed pages.
  2. Code number given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  3. Please check that this question paper contains 36 questions.
  4. Please write down the Serial Number of the question in the answer-book before attempting it.
  5. 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the students will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answerbook during this period.

MATHEMATICS

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:80

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This question paper comprises four Sections A, B, C and D. This question paper carries 36 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. Section A - Questions no. 1 to 20 comprises of 20 questions of 1 mark each.
  3. Section B - Questions no. 21 to 26 comprises of 6 questions of 2 marks each.
  4. Section C - Questions no. 27 to 32 comprises of 6 questions of 4 marks each.
  5. Section D - Questions no. 33 to 36 comprises of 4 questions of 6 marks each.
  6. There is no overall choice in the question paper. However, an internal choice has been provided in 3 questions of one mark, 2 questions of two marks, 2 questions of four marks and 2 questions of six marks. Only one of the choices in such questions have to be attempted.
  7. In addition to this, separate instructions are given with each section and question, wherever necessary.
  8. Use of calculators is not permitted.

SECTION A

Question numbers 1 to 20 carry 1 mark each.
Question numbers 1 to 10 are multiple choice type questions. Select the correct option.
1.

The value of p for which {tex}p(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} is a unit vector is

a. 0
b. {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
c. 1
d. {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}
2.

{tex}\left(\tan ^{-1} \frac{7}{9}+\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{8}\right){/tex} is equal to

a. {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{65}{72}\right){/tex}
b. {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{63}{65}\right){/tex}
c. {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex}
d. {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex}
3.

The feasible region for an LPP is shown below:
Let z = 3x - 4y be the objective function. Minimum of z occurs at

a. (0, 8)
b. (0, 0)
c. (5, 0)
d. (4, 10)
4.

If f and g are two functions from R to R defined as f(x) = |x| + x and g(x) = |x| – x, then fog (x) for x < 0 is:

a. 4x
b. 2x
c. 0
d. -4x
5.

{tex}\int \text e^\text x\left(\frac{\text x \log \text x+1}{\text x}\right)\text {dx}{/tex} is equal to

a. log (ex log x) + c
b. {tex}\frac{\text e^\text x}{\text x}+\text c{/tex}
c. x log x + ex + c
d. ex log x + c
6.

The integrating factor of the differential equation (x + 3y2){tex}\frac {\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = y is

a. y
b. -y
c. {tex}\frac {1}{\text y}{/tex}
d. {tex}-\frac {1}{\text y}{/tex}
7.

{tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then the value of |adj A| is

a. 64
b. 16
c. 0
d. -8
8.

The distance of the point (2, 3, 4) from the plane {tex}\vec{\text r} \cdot(3 \hat{\text i}-6 \hat{\text j}+2 \hat{\text k}){/tex} = -11 is

a. 0 unit
b. 1 unit
c. 2 units
d. {tex}\frac {15}{7}{/tex} units
9.

The maximum value of slope of the curve y = -x3 + 3x2 + 12x - 5 is:

a. 15
b. 12
c. 9
d. 0
10.

The vector equation of XY-plane is

a. {tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} = 0
b. {tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{j}}{/tex} = 0
c. {tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{i}}{/tex} = 0
d. {tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{n}}{/tex} = 1

Fill in the blanks in question numbers 11 to 15.
11.

The area of the parallelogram whose diagonals are {tex}2 \hat i{/tex} and {tex}-3 \hat k{/tex} is ________ square units.

OR

The value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} for which the vectors {tex}2 \hat{i}-\lambda \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} are orthogonal is ________.
12.

A bag contains 3 black, 4 red and 2 green balls. If three balls are drawn simultaneously at random, then the probability that the balls are of different colours is ________.
13.

The absolute minimum value of f(x) = 2 sin x in {tex}\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right]{/tex} is ________.
14.

If y = tan–1 x + cot–1x, x {tex}\in {/tex} R, then {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex}is equal to ________.

OR

If cos (xy) = k, where k is a constant and xy {tex}\ne{/tex} n{tex}\pi{/tex}, n {tex}\in{/tex} Z, then {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex}is equal to ________.
15.

The value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} so that the function f defined by:
{tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{lll} \lambda x, & \text { if } & x \leq \pi \\ \cos x, & \text { if } & x>\pi \end{array}\right.{/tex}
is continuous at x = {tex}\pi{/tex} is ________.

Question numbers 16 to 20 are very short answer type questions.
16.

Evaluate: {tex}\int\limits_{-2}^{2}|\text x| {/tex} dx

OR

Find: {tex}\int \frac{\text {dx}}{9+4 \text x^{2}}{/tex}
17.

Find the interval in which the function f given by f(x) = 7 - 4x - x2 is strictly increasing.
18.

Differentiate sin2({tex}\sqrt x{/tex}) with respect to x.
19.

Construct a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix A = [aij] whose elements are given by aij = |(i)2 - j|.
20.

A problem is given to three students whose probabilities of solving it are {tex}\frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac 16{/tex} respectively. If the events of solving the problem are independent, find the probability that at least one of them solves it.

SECTION B

Question numbers 21 to 26 carry 2 marks each.
21.

Show that for any two non-zero vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex}and {tex}\vec b{/tex}.
{tex}|\vec{a}+\vec{b}|=|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|{/tex} iff {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} are perpendicular vectors.

OR

Show that the vectors {tex}2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}, {tex}3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}5 \hat{\text i}+6 \hat{\text j}+2 \hat{\text k}{/tex} form the sides of a right-angled triangle.
22.

Find (AB)-1 if A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 0 \\ -4 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B-1 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 5 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}.
23.

If x = a sec {tex}\theta{/tex}, y = b tan {tex}\theta{/tex}, then find {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} at {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac \pi 3{/tex}.
24.

If A and B are two events such that P(A) = 0.4, P(B) = 0.3 and P(A {tex}\cup{/tex} B) = 0.6, then find P(B' {tex}\cap{/tex} A).
25.

Solve for x : {tex}\sin ^{-1} 4 x+\sin ^{-1} 3 x=-\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}

OR

Express {tex}\tan ^{-1}( \frac{\cos x}{1-\sin x}),-\frac{3 \pi}{2}<x<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} in the simplest form.
26.

Find the coordinates of the point where the line through (-1, 1, -8) and (5, -2, 10) crosses the ZX-plane.

SECTION C

Question numbers 27 to 32 carry 4 marks each.
27.

Solve the following LPP graphically:
Minimise z = 5x + 7y
subject to the constraints
2x + y {tex}\ge{/tex}8
x + 2y {tex}\ge{/tex} 10
x, y {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
28.

Evaluate: {tex}\int\limits_{-1}^2{/tex} |x3 - x| dx
29.

A bag contains two coins, one biased and the other unbiased. When tossed, the biased coin has a 60% chance of showing heads. One of the coins is selected at random and on tossing it shows tails. What is the probability it was an unbiased coin?

OR

The probability distribution of a random variable X, where k is a constant is given below:
{tex}\text P(\text X =\text x)=\left\{\begin{array}{lll} 0 \cdot 1, & \text { if } & \text x=0 \\ \text {kx}^{2}, & \text { if } & \text x=1 \\ \text {kx}, & \text { if } & \text x=2 \text { or } 3 \\ 0, & \text { otherwise } \end{array}\right.{/tex}
Determine

  1. the value of k
  2. P(x {tex}\le {/tex} 2)
  3. Mean of the variable X.
30.

Find the particular solution of the differential equation
cos y dx + (1 + e-x) sin y dy = 0
given that y = {tex}\frac {\pi}{4}{/tex} when x = 0.
31.

Show that the function f: ({tex}-\infty{/tex}, 0) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (-1, 0) defined by {tex}f(x)=\frac{x}{1+|x|}{/tex}, x {tex}\in{/tex} ({tex}-\infty{/tex}, 0) is one-one and onto.

OR

Show that the relation R in the set A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} given by R = {(a, b) : |a – b| is divisible by 2} is an equivalence relation.
32.

If y = x3 (cos x)x + sin-1{tex}\sqrt x{/tex}, find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}.

SECTION D

Question numbers 33 to 36 carry 6 marks each.
33.

Find the points on the curve 9y2 = x3, where the normal to the curve makes equal intercepts with both the axes. Also, find the equation of the normals.
34.

Show that the lines
{tex}\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{1}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y-3}{4}=\frac{z-4}{2}{/tex} intersect.
Also, find the coordinates of the point of intersection. Find the equation of the plane containing the two lines.
35.

Using integration, find the area of the region bounded by the lines x - y = 0, 3x - y = 0 and x + y = 12.

OR

Using integration, find the smaller area enclosed by the circle x2 + y2 = 4 and the line x + y = 2.
36.

Solve the following system of equations by matrix method :
x - y + 2z = 7
2x - y + 3z = 12
3x + 2y - z = 5

OR

Obtain the inverse of the following matrix using elementary operations:
A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 1 & -3 \\ -1 & -1 & 4 \\ 3 & 0 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

a. 0
b. {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
c. 1
d. {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}
Correct Answer: (b) {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
Explanation:

Let, vector {tex}p(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}
Magnitude of {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} is {tex}|\vec{a}|{/tex}
{tex}|\vec{a}|=\sqrt{(p)^{2}+(p)^{2}+(p)^{2}}=\pm \sqrt{3} p{/tex}
As {tex}\vec a{/tex} is a unit vector,
{tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}|\vec{a}|{/tex} = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\pm \sqrt{3} p=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}p=\pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}

2.

a. {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{65}{72}\right){/tex}
b. {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{63}{65}\right){/tex}
c. {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex}
d. {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex}
Correct Answer: (c) {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex} \tan ^{-1} \frac{7}{9}+\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{8} {/tex}
{tex} \tan ^{-1} x+\tan ^{-1} y=\tan ^{-1} \frac{x+y}{1-x y} . {/tex}
{tex} =\tan ^{-1} \frac{\frac{7}{9}+\frac{1}{8}}{1-\frac{7}{9} \cdot \frac{1}{8} .} {/tex}
{tex} =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{56+9}{72}}{\frac{72-7}{72}}\right) {/tex}
{tex} =\tan ^{-1} \frac{65}{72} \times \frac{72}{65} {/tex}
{tex} =\tan ^{-1} 1 . {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{\pi}{4} .{/tex}

a. (0, 8)
b. (0, 0)
c. (5, 0)
d. (4, 10)
Correct Answer: (a) (0, 8)
Explanation:
Corner points Z = 3x - 4y
(0, 0) 0
(5,0) 15
(6,8)  -14
(6 ,5) -2
(4,10) -28
(0,8) -32(Min.)

The minimum value occurs at (0,8)

    Show that the function f: ({tex}-\infty{/tex}, 0) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (-1, 0) defined by {tex}f(x)=\frac{x}{1+|x|}{/tex}, x {tex}\in{/tex} ({tex}-\infty{/tex}, 0) is one-one and onto.

    Solution:

    Let x1, x2 {tex}\in(-\infty, 0){/tex} such that f(x1) = f(x2)
    i.e., {tex}\frac{\text x_1}{1+\left|\text x_1\right|}=\frac{\text x_2}{1+\left|\text x_2\right|}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text x_1}{1-\text x_1} =\frac{\text x_2}{1-\text x_2}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 - x1x2 = x2 - x1x2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 = x2
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} f is one-one.
    Let y {tex}\in{/tex} (-1, 0) such that y = {tex}\frac{\text x}{1+|\text x|}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{\text x}{1-\text x}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{\text y}{1+\text y}{/tex}
    For each y {tex}\in{/tex} (-1, 0), there exists x {tex}\in{/tex} ({tex}-\infty{/tex}, 0),
    such that f(x) = {tex}\text f\left(\frac{\text y}{1+\text y}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{\text y}{1+\text y}}{1+\left|\frac{\text y}{1+\text y}\right|}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{\frac{\text y}{1+\text y}}{1-\frac{\text y}{1+\text y}}{/tex} = y

    Show that the relation R in the set A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} given by R = {(a, b) : |a – b| is divisible by 2} is an equivalence relation.

    Solution:

    Reflexive: |a - a| = 0, which is divisible by 2 for all a {tex}\in{/tex} A.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} (a, a) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is reflexive.
    Symmetric: Let (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R i.e., |a - b| = {tex}2 \lambda{/tex}, {tex}\lambda \in \omega{/tex}
    then |b - a| = |-(a - b)| = |a - b| = {tex}2 \lambda{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b, a) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is symmetric.
    Transitive: Let (a, b), (b, c) {tex}\in{/tex} R i.e., |a - b| = {tex}2 \lambda{/tex}, |b - c| = {tex}2 \mu{/tex}
    a - c = (a - b) + (b - c) = {tex}\pm 2 \lambda \pm 2 \mu{/tex} = {tex}\pm 2(\lambda+\mu){/tex}
    |a - c| = {tex}2|\lambda+\mu|{/tex}, which is divisible by 2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a, c) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is transitive.
    Hence R is an equivalence relation.

    33.

    Find the points on the curve 9y2 = x3, where the normal to the curve makes equal intercepts with both the axes. Also, find the equation of the normals.

    Solution:

    Given curve is {tex}9y^2=x^3{/tex}...(i)
    On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
    {tex} 9 \times 2 y \frac { d y } { d x } = 3 x ^ { 2 }{/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 3 x ^ { 2 } } { 18 y } = \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 6 y }{/tex}
    Let (x1, y1) be the required point on the curve (i).
    Then, the slope of normal to the curve (i) at point (x1, y1)
    Since normal makes equal intercept on the axes.
    {tex} = - \frac { 1 } { ( d y / d x ) _ { \left( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } \right) } } = \frac { - 6 y _ { 1 } } { x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } }{/tex}...(ii)
    Since normal makes equal intercepts on the axes.
    {tex} \therefore{/tex}Slope of normal ={tex}\tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } , \tan \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 } = \pm 1{/tex} 
    Therefore, {tex}\frac { - 6 y _ { 1 } } { x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } }=\pm1{/tex}
    Hence, {tex}y _ { 1 } = \pm \frac { x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { 6 }{/tex} ...(iii)
    Again, point (x1, y1) lies on curve (i)
    {tex}\therefore \quad 9 y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = x _ { 1 } ^ { 3 }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow 9 \left( \pm \frac { x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { 6 } \right) ^ { 2 } = x _ { 1 } ^ { 3 }{/tex} [Using Eq.(iii)]
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 9 x _ { 1 } ^ { 4 } } { 36 } = x _ { 1 } ^ { 3 }{/tex} 
    Note that x1 {tex} \ne{/tex} 0, as if x1 = 0, then y1 = 0. So, normal will pass through (0, 0), which is not possible.
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x _ { 1 } } { 4 } = 1{/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow x _ { 1 } = 4{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore y _ { 1 } = \pm \frac { x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { 6 } = \pm \frac { 16 } { 6 } = \pm \frac { 8 } { 3 }{/tex}
    Hence, the required points are {tex}\left( 4 , \frac { 8 } { 3 } \right) \text { and } \left( 4 , \frac { - 8 } { 3 } \right){/tex}{tex}{/tex}
    Equation of normal at {tex}\left(4, \frac{8}{3}\right){/tex} is {tex}y-\frac{8}{3}{/tex} = −(x − 4)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x + 3y + 20 = 0
    and equation of normal at {tex}\left(4,-\frac{8}{3}\right){/tex} is {tex}\mathrm{y}+\frac{8}{3}{/tex}= −(x − 4)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x + 3y = 20

    Show that the lines
    {tex}\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{1}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y-3}{4}=\frac{z-4}{2}{/tex} intersect.
    Also, find the coordinates of the point of intersection. Find the equation of the plane containing the two lines.

    Solution:

    {tex}\frac{\text x-2}{1}=\frac{\text y-2}{3}=\frac{\text z-3}{1}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda{/tex}(say)
    and {tex}\frac{\text x-2}{1}=\frac{\text y-3}{3}=\frac{\text z-4}{2}=\mu{/tex}(say)
    Arbitrary points on the lines are
    {tex}(\lambda+2,3 \lambda+2, \lambda+3){/tex} and {tex}(\mu+2,4 \mu+3,2 \mu+4){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda + 2{/tex} = {tex}\mu + 2{/tex}, and {tex}\lambda + 3{/tex} = {tex}2\mu + 4{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\mu{/tex}, solving we get {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -1, {tex}\mu{/tex} = -1
    {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -1, {tex}\mu{/tex} = -1 satisfying y-coordinates {tex}3\lambda{/tex} + 2 = {tex}4\mu{/tex} + 3
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Point of intersection is (1, -1, 2)
    Equation of plane passing through two given lines are
    {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \text x-2 & \text y-2 & \text z-3 \\ 1 & 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 4 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x - y + z - 5 = 0

    36.

    Solve the following system of equations by matrix method :
    x - y + 2z = 7
    2x - y + 3z = 12
    3x + 2y - z = 5

    Solution:

    Writing given equations in matrix form
    {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 2 & -1 & 3 \\ 3 & 2 & -1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} \text x \\ \text y \\ \text z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 7 \\ 12 \\ 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    Which is of the form AX = B
    Here |A| = -2 {tex}\neq{/tex} 0
    A-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{-2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -5 & 3 & -1 \\ 11 & -7 & 1 \\ 7 & -5 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} X = A-1B = {tex}\frac{1}{-2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -5 & 3 & -1 \\ 11 & -7 & 1 \\ 7 & -5 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 7 \\ 12 \\ 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2, y = 1, z = 3

    A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 1 & -3 \\ -1 & -1 & 4 \\ 3 & 0 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

    Solution:

    Using elementary row transformation,
    A = IA {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 1 & -3 \\ -1 & -1 & 4 \\ 3 & 0 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \cdot \mathrm{A}{/tex}
    Operating R1 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R1 + R2
    {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 & 4 \\ 3 & 0 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \mathrm{A}{/tex}
    R2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R2 + R1, R3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R3 - 3R1
    {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 & 5 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \\ -3 & -3 & 1 \end{array}\right] \cdot \mathrm{A}{/tex}
    R2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} -R2
    {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & -5 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & -2 & 0 \\ -3 & -3 & 1 \end{array}\right] \cdot \mathrm{A}{/tex}
    R1 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R1 + R3, R2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R2 - 5R3
    {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -2 & -2 & 1 \\ 14 & 13 & -5 \\ -3 & -3 & 1 \end{array}\right] \cdot A{/tex}
    R3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} -R3
    {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -2 & -2 & 1 \\ 14 & 13 & -5 \\ 3 & 3 & -1 \end{array}\right] \cdot \text A{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A-1 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -2 & -2 & 1 \\ 14 & 13 & -5 \\ 3 & 3 & -1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
